local_names.tamil: நியான் காவ் வறுத்தது (முட்டை தோய்த்து வறுத்த அரிசி கேக்)
local_names.hindi: तले हुए नियान गाओ (अंडे में डुबोकर तला हुआ चावल केक)

region_of_origin: Singapore (Chinese New Year tradition, originating from China)
food_subgroup: Glutinous rice cake, egg-battered and pan-fried; sliced approximately 1 cm thick, dipped in beaten egg, pan-fried in a small amount of oil

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for egg-battered pan-fried nian gao. Closest parent concept is fried glutinous rice cake (FOODON_03301710 — glutinous rice cake). No exact URI can be confirmed without live database access; null is used to maintain ontology honesty.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice cake — closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for glutinous rice cake preparations. Code A0CKP (Rice cake) is used as a closest-parent match. No specific egg-battered fried nian gao code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for egg-battered pan-fried nian gao. A0CKP (Rice cake) is the closest defensible parent. The egg coating and pan-frying preparation are not captured in this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for nian gao or egg-battered fried glutinous rice cake. SNOMED CT food coding is limited for Asian traditional foods.

serving.description: 3 slices (approximately 120g total: ~90g nian gao base + egg coating + oil absorbed)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is 3 slices of egg-battered pan-fried nian gao, weighing approximately 120g in total, providing 384 kcal, 72g carbohydrate, and 8.2g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Nian Gao Egg-Battered Fried — HPB Singapore Foodo component analysis (nian gao base + egg + oil)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo was used as the primary regional source for this Singapore Chinese dish. Values are derived from component analysis of the nian gao base (glutinous rice flour + brown sugar), beaten egg coating, and pan-frying oil absorption. HPB Foodo is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — glutinous rice and rice cake analogues; plain nian gao GI ~71 (Atkinson et al., 2008); egg-battered fried version estimated ~65 based on fat and protein moderating effect
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain nian gao (glutinous rice cake) has a published GI of approximately 71 (high) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international tables. The egg-battered and pan-fried version is estimated at GI ~65 (medium-high boundary) because the egg coating adds protein and fat, which slow gastric emptying and blunt the glycemic response compared to plain steamed nian gao. This is a defensible analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for egg-battered pan-fried nian gao was found in the approved GI literature. Glycemic load per 120g serving = (65 × 72g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 46.8 (very high).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (30 mg per 100g; 36 mg per 120g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Egg-battered pan-fried nian gao is very low in sodium (36 mg per standard 3-slice serving), posing no meaningful sodium burden for individuals managing hypertension, provided it is not served with high-sodium condiments or sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high glycemic load per serving (GL ~47) driven by glutinous rice flour base (almost entirely amylopectin starch) and very high sugar content (50g per 100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: A standard 3-slice serving (120g) delivers approximately 72g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~47 — equivalent to the glycemic impact of approximately 2.5 bowls of white rice — making this food unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; if consumed at all during Chinese New Year, a single slice (40g, GL ~16) as a symbolic tasting portion is the maximum advisable amount.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate cholesterol (75 mg/100g from egg), low saturated fat (1.5g/100g), very high sugar content promoting triglyceride elevation and AGE formation with regular consumption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While saturated fat is low (1.5g/100g) and sodium is minimal, the very high sugar content (50g/100g) poses a cardiovascular risk through elevated triglycerides, insulin resistance, and advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation if consumed regularly; occasional CNY consumption of 1–2 slices is unlikely to be harmful for most individuals with cardiovascular disease, but frequent intake should be avoided.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar content promotes pro-inflammatory AGE formation and glycation pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The very high sugar content (50g/100g) in nian gao may contribute to systemic inflammation via advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit consumption to occasional small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg-Battered Pan-Fried Nian Gao (煎年糕, Jiān Nián Gāo) is one of Singapore's most beloved Chinese New Year home preparations. The traditional CNY sticky rice cake — nian gao (年糕, literally 'year cake', a homophone for 'year high' or 'progressing year') — is made from glutinous rice flour and brown sugar, steamed into a dense, sticky, deeply sweet block. For the pan-fried version, the cold, hardened cake is sliced approximately 1 cm thick, dipped in beaten egg to form a coating, and pan-fried in a small amount of oil until a golden, slightly crispy egg crust forms around the molten, chewy, caramel-sweet interior. The contrast between the crisp egg shell and the soft, elastic, intensely sweet glutinous rice interior is the defining sensory characteristic of this preparation. Nutritionally, a 3-slice serving (120g) provides approximately 384 kcal, 72g carbohydrate, 60g of which is sugar, 8.2g fat, and 7.8g protein. The glycemic load per serving is approximately 47 — among the highest of any common Singaporean snack food — driven by the almost entirely amylopectin starch of glutinous rice (GI ~65–71) and the very high brown sugar content. The egg coating marginally lowers the GI compared to plain steamed nian gao (~65 vs ~71) by adding protein and fat that slow gastric emptying. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, this food is a high-risk item and should be limited to a single slice as a symbolic CNY gesture. Sodium is very low (30 mg/100g), making it non-concerning for hypertension. The egg contributes cholesterol (75 mg/100g), vitamin A, riboflavin, selenium, and vitamin B12. Also known as kuih bakul (Malay), lin gou (Hokkien), and tinn kue (Teochew).
common_user_queries: pan fried nian gao calories per slice
meal_context: Chinese New Year dessert or snack

confidence_reason: HPB Singapore Foodo is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. However, the nutrient values are derived from component analysis (nian gao base + egg + oil) rather than a direct laboratory measurement of the finished egg-battered pan-fried product. The GI value is an analogous estimate based on published plain nian gao GI (~71) adjusted for the moderating effect of egg protein and fat, not a direct measurement. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g/100g — confirmed zero by formulation (no hydrogenated fats used; cooking oil and egg are the only fat sources). Vitamin C is reported as 0.0 mg/100g — naturally zero, as glutinous rice flour, brown sugar, egg, and cooking oil contain no meaningful vitamin C. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown is not available from the component analysis source and is set to -1. Water content is not reported by the source and is set to -1. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; the closest published GI for plain nian gao is ~71 (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008). The egg-battered fried version is estimated at ~65 based on the established principle that added protein and fat slow gastric emptying and reduce glycemic response. Glycemic load per 120g serving = (65 × 60.0 g carbohydrate per 100g × 1.2) / 100 = 46.8. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Rice cake) is used as the closest defensible parent; no specific code for egg-battered pan-fried nian gao exists. FoodOn URI is null pending live database verification. This record covers the egg-battered pan-fried preparation only; plain steamed nian gao is a separate record.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Added 'நியான் காவ் வறுத்தது' and 'தலே ஹுயே நியான் காஓ' to the flat 'aliases' list to address Q8 failure from iteration 1.

Iteration 3: Audited all aliases_by_language entries against flat aliases list to ensure complete bidirectional consistency. Confirmed all Tamil entries (நியான் காவ் (சர்க்கரை அரிசி கேக்), நியான் காவ் (Nian Gao), நியான் காவ் வறுத்தது, தலே ஹுயே நியான் காஓ), all Hindi entries (तले हुए नियான गाओ, नियान गाओ), all Malay entries (Nian Gao Goreng Telur, Kuih Bakul / Nian Gao, Kuih Bakul Goreng), all Chinese entries (煎年糕, 年糕, 炸年糕), and all romanized entries (Jiān Nián Gāo, Jian Nian Gao, Niángāo, Nin4 gou1) are present in the flat aliases list. Added 'Jian Nian Gao' and 'Kuih Bakul Goreng' to flat aliases for full consistency.
